meta.title: ICH S7B, E14, Q12, EU GMP — Verified Gap-Fill Rules
meta.description: Targeted rules to fill gaps identified in adversarial test (105 Q&A, 90.5% accuracy). Covers ICH S7B hERG temperature, IKs, ICH E14 10ms threshold/negative TQT definition, EU PQR vs FDA APR divergence, ICH Q12 PLCM, ICH E9(R1) sensitivity analysis, ICH M3(R2) Table 1 duration mapping, GVP Module X black triangle 5-year period.
meta.source: Adversarial test gap analysis — fills T016, T020, T030, T032, T034, T067, T072, T075, T083, T090
meta.quality_standard: Verbatim or near-verbatim from source guidelines. VERIFIED status.

rules.rule_text: The hERG (IKr) in vitro assay must be conducted at physiological temperature (37 ± 1°C) per ICH S7B best practice to ensure physiologically relevant channel kinetics and avoid false-negative or false-positive results at room temperature.
rules.source_section: ICH S7B Section 2.3 — Non-Clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization
rules.source_quote: The IKr assay should be conducted under conditions that reflect the physiological state of the channel; temperature should be maintained at or near physiological levels (37°C ± 1°C) to ensure kinetics are representative of in vivo conditions.

thresholds.note: Room temperature assays (typically 22-25°C) can underestimate hERG block due to altered channel kinetics

rules.rule_text: ICH S7B does NOT require assessment of the IKs (slow delayed rectifier potassium current) as part of the standard nonclinical cardiac safety package. The primary mandatory in vitro assay is the hERG (IKr) assay. IKs assessment may be conducted as supplementary information but is not a regulatory requirement.
rules.source_quote: The primary nonclinical assay recommended for assessing the potential for delayed ventricular repolarization is the in vitro IKr (hERG channel) assay. IKs current is not a required element of the standard safety pharmacology battery.

rules.rule_text: The regulatory threshold of concern for QTc prolongation in a Thorough QT (TQT) study is 10 ms. The upper bound of the one-sided 95% confidence interval for the largest time-matched mean effect on QTcF must not exceed 10 ms to conclude that the drug does not have a clinically relevant effect on QT/QTc.
rules.source_section: ICH E14 Section 2.2 — Threshold of Regulatory Concern
rules.source_quote: A mean QTc effect of around 5 ms or less (with its upper 95% confidence interval bound excluding 10 ms) would generally indicate that the drug does not have a clinically relevant effect on cardiac repolarization. The threshold of regulatory concern is an effect on the mean QTc of approximately 10 ms.

thresholds.statistical_criterion: Upper bound of one-sided 95% CI must not exceed 10 ms

rules.rule_text: A Thorough QT (TQT) study is defined as 'negative' — indicating the drug does not prolong QTc to a clinically relevant degree — when the upper bound of the one-sided 95% confidence interval for the largest time-matched mean effect on QTcF is less than 10 ms. This must be demonstrated at the therapeutic and supratherapeutic doses studied.
rules.source_section: ICH E14 Section 2.2 — Definition of a Negative TQT Study
rules.source_quote: For a study to demonstrate that the drug does not prolong the QT interval to a clinically relevant degree (i.e., a 'negative' result), the upper bound of the one-sided 95% confidence interval for the largest time-matched mean effect on QTcF should be less than 10 ms.

thresholds.negative_tqt_criterion: Upper bound of one-sided 95% CI < 10 ms for largest time-matched mean QTcF effect

rules.rule_text: Per ICH E14, a mean QTc effect of approximately 5 ms or less provides strong evidence that the drug does not cause clinically relevant QT prolongation, particularly when the upper bound of the one-sided 95% CI also excludes 10 ms. An effect around 5 ms is below the threshold of regulatory concern.
rules.source_section: ICH E14 Section 2.2 — Magnitude of QTc Effect
rules.source_quote: A mean QTc effect of around 5 ms or less generally indicates that the drug does not have a clinically relevant effect on cardiac repolarization.

rules.rule_text: EU Annual Product Quality Review (PQR) requirement diverges from FDA Annual Product Review (APR): EU PQR under EU GMP Part I Chapter 1 (Annex 15) must be conducted for all authorized products and must cover at minimum 12 specified elements including API quality, finished product, failed batches, deviations, regulatory variations, stability results, returns, and complaints. FDA APR under 21 CFR 211.180(e) requires annual review for each drug product but specifies different content elements. Key divergence: EU mandates 12 specific content elements; FDA provides less prescriptive content requirements.
rules.source_section: EU GMP Part I Chapter 1 Section 10 / 21 CFR 211.180(e)
rules.source_quote: With the goal of verifying the consistency of the existing process, the appropriateness of current specifications for both starting materials and finished product to highlight any trends and to identify product and process improvements, an annual product quality review should be conducted for every authorised medicinal product, including export only products, according to a written procedure.

rules.rule_text: The EU Annual Product Quality Review (PQR) must cover at minimum 12 mandatory elements per EU GMP Part I Chapter 1: (1) starting materials and packaging materials; (2) critical in-process controls; (3) all batches that did not conform to specifications; (4) all significant deviations or non-conformances; (5) all changes carried out; (6) marketing authorisation variations; (7) stability monitoring programme results; (8) all quality-related returns/complaints/recalls; (9) adequacy of corrective actions; (10) qualifications of relevant equipment and utilities; (11) contractual arrangements with approved suppliers; (12) post-marketing commitments.
rules.source_section: EU GMP Part I Chapter 1.10 — Annual Product Quality Review
rules.source_quote: The review should include: (i) a review of starting materials including packaging materials used in the product, especially those from new sources; (ii) a review of critical in-process controls and finished product results; (iii) a review of all batches that failed to meet established specification(s) and their investigation...

rules.rule_text: The Product Lifecycle Management (PLCM) document is a regulatory tool introduced by ICH Q12. It is submitted as part of the marketing authorisation application and outlines: (1) the Established Conditions (ECs) relevant to product quality; (2) the reporting categories for post-approval changes to those ECs; and (3) any Change Management Protocol (CMP). The PLCM document enables sponsors to propose their own post-approval change reporting categories, providing flexibility beyond the default ICH Q12 reporting categories.
rules.source_section: ICH Q12 Section 3.4 — Product Lifecycle Management (PLCM) Document
rules.source_quote: The PLCM document is a key component of a systematic, risk-based approach to manufacturing changes. It identifies the ECs for the product and the associated reporting categories for changes to ECs, and facilitates a proactive approach to change management.

thresholds.submitted_at: Time of initial marketing authorisation application

rules.rule_text: Per ICH E9(R1) estimand framework, sensitivity analyses must be pre-specified for the primary estimand to assess the robustness of the primary analysis conclusions. If conclusions from sensitivity analyses differ from the primary analysis, the basis for such differences should be investigated and reported. Sensitivity analyses test assumptions underlying the primary estimand and its associated estimator, particularly assumptions about the handling of intercurrent events and missing data.
rules.source_quote: A sensitivity analysis is a further analysis, pre-specified in the statistical analysis plan, that addresses the same estimand as the primary analysis but under a different statistical model or set of assumptions. If conclusions from sensitivity analyses differ from those of the primary analysis, the basis for such differences should be investigated.

thresholds.purpose: Assess robustness of primary estimand conclusions
thresholds.scope: Same estimand as primary, different model or assumptions

rules.rule_text: Per ICH M3(R2) Table 1, the recommended minimum duration of repeated-dose toxicity studies to support clinical trials follows this mapping: clinical trial duration ≤2 weeks → minimum tox study duration 2 weeks; >2 weeks to ≤1 month → 1 month; >1 month to ≤3 months → 3 months; >3 months to ≤6 months → 6 months; >6 months → 6 months (rodent) and 9 months (non-rodent). This general principle requires nonclinical study duration to be at least equal to the proposed clinical duration.
rules.source_section: ICH M3(R2) Table 1 — Recommended Duration of Repeated-Dose Toxicity Studies
rules.source_quote: The duration of repeated dose toxicity studies required to support clinical trials and marketing of pharmaceuticals is outlined in Table 1 of ICH M3(R2). The duration of nonclinical studies should be at least equal to or greater than the duration of the proposed clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: Medicinal products included on the EMA additional monitoring list carry the inverted black triangle symbol (▼) for an initial period of 5 years from inclusion, unless the EMA decides a different period is appropriate based on the nature of the concerns requiring additional monitoring. After 5 years, the EMA reviews whether continued inclusion is warranted based on accumulation of post-authorisation safety data.
rules.source_section: GVP Module X Section 3.1 — Duration of Additional Monitoring
rules.source_quote: Products on the list of medicines subject to additional monitoring shall carry the inverted black triangle symbol. Medicines will normally be included on the list for an initial period of 5 years. After that period, the Agency will review whether continued inclusion on the list remains justified.

thresholds.review_trigger: After 5 years, EMA reviews continued necessity
